cuisine: American (used globally as a condiment; served at Sukiya Japan)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Tabasco brand sauce; 'hot sauce' (FOODON_03301710) is the closest defensible broader class. Match type set to closest_parent as Tabasco sauce is a specific instance of the hot sauce category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers hot/chilli sauces broadly; no Tabasco-specific code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue. Match type set to closest_parent as Tabasco sauce is a specific branded product within this category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to Tabasco sauce as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Tabasco sauce is approximately 5 grams (about 1 teaspoon or a few dashes), as it is used sparingly as a condiment or flavour enhancer.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Tabasco Brand Pepper Sauce (Branded Food)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central Branded Food entry for Tabasco Original Red Pepper Sauce (McIlhenny Co.) used as the primary nutrient reference. Values per 100 g derived from the USDA entry. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors: protein 1 g × 4 + carbohydrate 2 g × 4 + fat 0.2 g × 9 = 13.8 kcal; USDA reports approximately 12–14 kcal/100 g consistent with this calculation. Sodium 600 mg/100 g is consistent with USDA data for original Tabasco (approximately 590–620 mg/100 g). Minor rounding applied.

gi_source.title: N/A — Tabasco sauce contains negligible carbohydrates (2 g/100 g); GI testing is not applicable for condiments used in sub-5 g servings
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (consulted; no entry for Tabasco sauce)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Tabasco sauce is not listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. At a typical serving of 5 g, total carbohydrate intake is 0.1 g, making glycaemic load effectively zero. gi_category is set to not_applicable as the food contains negligible carbohydrates per serving. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = negligible; reported as 0.0. glycemic_index is set to 0 as a numeric sentinel consistent with not_applicable classification for this negligible-carbohydrate condiment.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content at 600 mg per 100 g; salt is a primary ingredient
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Tabasco sauce to 1–2 dashes (approximately 1–3 g) per meal, as even small volumes contribute meaningful sodium; cumulative sodium from all condiments must be tracked against the daily target of under 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2 g/100 g) and sugar (1 g/100 g); negligible glycaemic impact at typical serving sizes
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tabasco sauce is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes when used in typical condiment quantities (1–5 g per serving), as the carbohydrate and sugar content per serving is negligible and has no meaningful effect on blood glucose; however, flavoured or sweetened Tabasco variants should be checked for added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (600 mg/100 g) is the primary cardiovascular concern; fat and saturated fat are negligible
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Tabasco sauce should be used sparingly (1–5 g per occasion) to avoid excessive sodium accumulation; the fat profile is not a concern, but the high sodium density warrants caution in the context of a sodium-restricted cardiac diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red pepper is a nightshade vegetable; high sodium may worsen tissue swelling in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables (solanine alkaloids) should avoid or strictly limit Tabasco sauce; the high sodium content (600 mg/100 g) may also exacerbate RA-related tissue swelling even at small serving sizes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tabasco sauce is a commercially produced American hot sauce manufactured by McIlhenny Company on Avery Island, Louisiana, USA. It is made from only three ingredients: aged red Capsicum frutescens peppers, distilled vinegar, and salt. The peppers are mashed, mixed with salt, and aged in white oak barrels for up to three years before being blended with vinegar and strained into the final product. The sauce has a sharp, tangy, intensely spicy flavour with a thin, pourable consistency and a vivid red colour. It is used globally as a condiment and flavour enhancer, added in small quantities (typically 1–5 g or a few dashes) to dishes such as pizza, pasta, eggs, soups, and grilled meats. In Japan, Tabasco is commonly served as a condiment at pizza and fast-food chains including Sukiya. Nutritionally, Tabasco sauce is very low in energy (approximately 14 kcal per 100 g), protein (1 g), fat (0.2 g), and carbohydrate (2 g), making it essentially calorie-free at typical serving sizes. Its primary nutritional concern is its very high sodium content at approximately 600 mg per 100 g, which is significant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Because typical servings are only 1–5 g, the absolute sodium intake per use is low (6–30 mg), but cumulative use across meals can add up. The glycaemic index is not applicable given the negligible carbohydrate content per serving. Tabasco is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and those following gluten-free or halal diets. It is not suitable as a regular condiment for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its nightshade classification and high sodium density.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in Tabasco sauce?
Can I use Tabasco sauce if I have high blood pressure?
What are the ingredients in Tabasco sauce?
How many calories in Tabasco sauce?
Is Tabasco sauce suitable for rheumatoid arthritis?

タバスコ ナトリウム

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central Branded Food entry for Tabasco Original Red Pepper Sauce, which is an exact match for this commercially standardised product. Values are consistent with the input record and cross-validated against the known three-ingredient formulation.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Tabasco sauce is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central branded entry consulted as of 2026-04-17. 2. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and all remaining micronutrient fields are set to -1: these values are not reported in the USDA branded entry for Tabasco sauce at the level of detail required. 3. water_g is set to -1: moisture content not reported in the USDA branded entry. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; Tabasco sauce contains no animal-derived fat. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; no partially hydrogenated oils are used. 6. glycemic_index is set to 0 as a numeric sentinel value and gi_category is not_applicable: Tabasco sauce contains negligible carbohydrates (2 g/100 g) and is used in sub-5 g servings, making GI testing and GI classification not applicable. The value 0 is used here as a numeric placeholder consistent with the not_applicable classification, not as a measured GI value. 7. The input record described this as a 'Japanese beef dish' — this is incorrect; Tabasco is an American hot sauce condiment served at Sukiya Japan. The food_name, description, and cuisine have been corrected accordingly. 8. Input ethnic_tags ('Japanese Curry', 'Grilled Chicken Skewers') were irrelevant to Tabasco sauce and have been removed. 9. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not correspond to the HPB nutrient database and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the approved primary source. 10. Dietary tag 'halal' retained; 'kosher' not confirmed and set to false. 11. Energy in input was listed as 20 kcal/100 g; recalculation using Atwater factors yields approximately 14 kcal/100 g, which is consistent with USDA data; corrected to 14.0 kcal. 12. ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected from 'broad' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].
